text.title: AARRR Common Mistakes
text.summary: Five common failure modes when implementing AARRR metrics, with root causes and corrections.

antipattern.description: Tracking more than one metric per AARRR stage.
antipattern.reason: Multiple metrics per stage create analysis paralysis and diffuse ownership. Each stage needs one accountable number; secondary metrics are context, not targets.

antipattern.description: Focusing investment on acquisition when activation or retention is broken.
antipattern.reason: Every new user acquired into a broken funnel is waste multiplied. Fix the weakest stage first; the downstream revenue impact is proportional to acquisition volume.

antipattern.description: Defining activation as "created profile" or another setup action that does not predict retention.
antipattern.reason: Optimizing for setup completion inflates activation rate while the product's retention remains flat. The only valid activation definition is an action empirically correlated with D30 retention.

antipattern.description: Not segmenting AARRR metrics by acquisition channel or cohort.
antipattern.reason: Aggregate metrics mask variation. A strong paid-search cohort can hide poor organic retention; blending them produces a misleading average that leads to wrong investment decisions.

antipattern.description: Ignoring the referral stage until late in product maturity.
antipattern.reason: Referral programs take 3-6 months to generate meaningful signal. Starting instrumentation and basic mechanics early means the data is available when it is needed for scaling decisions.
